name: Cloud Computing Benefits
description: Core advantages of cloud computing including agility, elasticity, cost savings, and global reach
key_facts: Trade capital expense (CAPEX) for operational expense (OPEX)
Benefit from massive economies of scale
Stop guessing capacity — scale on demand
Increase speed and agility of deployment
Go global in minutes using AWS Regions
common_misconceptions: Cloud is always cheaper than on-premises (depends on usage patterns and optimization)
Cloud eliminates all IT responsibilities (shared responsibility model applies)

name: Cloud Deployment Models
description: Public cloud, private cloud, and hybrid cloud deployment models
key_facts: Public cloud: fully deployed on a cloud provider like AWS
Private cloud (on-premises): deployed using cloud-like technologies in a private data center
Hybrid cloud: combination of public and private cloud connected together
AWS supports all three models with services like Outposts for hybrid
common_misconceptions: Hybrid cloud means using two public cloud providers (it means public + private)
Private cloud means no cloud benefits (private clouds can still offer elasticity and automation)

name: Well-Architected Framework
description: AWS framework with six pillars for building secure, resilient, and efficient cloud architectures
key_facts: Six pillars: Operational Excellence, Security, Reliability, Performance Efficiency, Cost Optimization, Sustainability
AWS Well-Architected Tool helps review workloads against best practices
Each pillar has design principles and best practices
Used to evaluate and improve cloud architectures
common_misconceptions: The framework has only five pillars (Sustainability was added as the sixth pillar)
Well-Architected is a compliance requirement (it is a best-practice guidance framework)

name: AWS Global Infrastructure
description: Regions, Availability Zones, and Edge Locations that make up AWS global infrastructure
key_facts: A Region is a geographic area with multiple isolated data center groups
Each Region has at least 3 Availability Zones (AZs)
AZs are one or more discrete data centers with redundant power and networking
Edge Locations are used by CloudFront for content caching closer to users
Choose a Region based on compliance, latency, service availability, and pricing
common_misconceptions: All AWS services are available in every Region (service availability varies by Region)
Availability Zones are single data centers (each AZ can have multiple data centers)

name: Shared Responsibility Model
description: Division of security responsibilities between AWS and the customer
key_facts: AWS is responsible for security OF the cloud (hardware, software, networking, facilities)
Customer is responsible for security IN the cloud (data, IAM, OS patching, firewall config)
Shared controls include patch management, configuration management, and awareness training
Responsibility varies by service type: IaaS vs managed services
common_misconceptions: AWS handles all security (customers must secure their data and access)
Using managed services means zero security responsibility (customers still manage data and access)

name: IAM Basics
description: Identity and Access Management fundamentals including users, groups, roles, and policies
key_facts: Root account should be secured with MFA and not used for daily tasks
IAM users represent individual people or services
Groups are collections of users with shared permissions
Roles provide temporary credentials for services or cross-account access
Policies are JSON documents that define permissions (allow or deny)
common_misconceptions: IAM users need a password for programmatic access (they need access keys, not passwords)
Groups can be nested inside other groups (IAM groups cannot contain other groups)

name: MFA and Account Security
description: Multi-factor authentication and best practices for securing AWS accounts
key_facts: MFA adds a second layer of authentication beyond password
MFA should always be enabled on the root account
Supported MFA devices: virtual (app), hardware key fob, U2F security key
AWS recommends using least privilege principle for all IAM entities
common_misconceptions: MFA is only needed for the root account (all privileged users should have MFA)
A strong password alone is sufficient (MFA provides critical additional protection)

name: AWS Organizations
description: Centralized management and governance for multiple AWS accounts
key_facts: Manage multiple AWS accounts from a single management account
Consolidated billing aggregates usage for volume discounts
Service Control Policies (SCPs) set permission guardrails across accounts
Organizational Units (OUs) group accounts for policy application
common_misconceptions: SCPs grant permissions (they only restrict the maximum available permissions)
Each account must be billed separately (consolidated billing combines them)

name: AWS Compliance Programs
description: AWS compliance certifications and tools for meeting regulatory requirements
key_facts: AWS Artifact provides access to AWS compliance reports and agreements
AWS holds certifications like SOC, PCI DSS, HIPAA, ISO 27001
AWS Config tracks resource configurations for compliance auditing
CloudTrail logs API calls for governance and audit trails
common_misconceptions: AWS compliance means the customer is automatically compliant (customer must still ensure their workloads comply)
AWS Config and CloudTrail do the same thing (Config tracks resource state, CloudTrail tracks API calls)

name: EC2 Basics
description: Elastic Compute Cloud virtual server fundamentals
key_facts: EC2 provides resizable virtual servers in the cloud
Instance types vary by CPU, memory, storage, and networking (e.g., t3, m5, c5)
AMIs (Amazon Machine Images) are templates for launching instances
Security Groups act as virtual firewalls controlling inbound/outbound traffic
Key pairs are used for SSH access to Linux instances
common_misconceptions: EC2 instances run indefinitely for free (only the Free Tier offers limited free hours)
Stopping an instance deletes its data (EBS volumes persist when an instance stops)

name: S3 Basics
description: Simple Storage Service for scalable object storage
key_facts: S3 stores objects (files) in buckets
Objects can be up to 5 TB in size
Bucket names must be globally unique
S3 provides 99.999999999% (11 nines) durability
Storage classes include Standard, Standard-IA, Glacier for different access patterns
common_misconceptions: S3 is a file system (it is object storage, not block or file storage)
S3 bucket names can be reused across accounts (names are globally unique)

name: RDS and Database Basics
description: Managed relational database service supporting multiple database engines
key_facts: RDS supports MySQL, PostgreSQL, MariaDB, Oracle, SQL Server, and Aurora
AWS handles patching, backups, and high availability
Multi-AZ deployment provides automatic failover for high availability
Read Replicas improve read performance
DynamoDB is the AWS NoSQL (key-value) database alternative
common_misconceptions: RDS allows SSH access to the database server (it is a managed service with no OS-level access)
DynamoDB and RDS serve the same purpose (RDS is relational, DynamoDB is NoSQL)

name: Lambda and Serverless Basics
description: Serverless compute service that runs code without provisioning servers
key_facts: No servers to provision or manage
Pay only for compute time consumed (per-millisecond billing)
Automatically scales from zero to thousands of concurrent executions
Maximum execution time is 15 minutes per invocation
Supports multiple languages: Python, Node.js, Java, Go, .NET, Ruby
common_misconceptions: Lambda can run indefinitely (15-minute maximum timeout)
Lambda is always cheaper than EC2 (for steady-state workloads, EC2 can be cheaper)

name: VPC and Networking Basics
description: Virtual Private Cloud and core networking concepts in AWS
key_facts: VPC is a logically isolated virtual network within AWS
Subnets divide a VPC across Availability Zones
Public subnets have routes to an Internet Gateway
Private subnets use NAT Gateways for outbound internet access
Security Groups and NACLs control traffic at different levels
common_misconceptions: All subnets are public by default (subnets are private unless routed to an Internet Gateway)
VPCs span multiple regions (a VPC exists within a single region)

name: CloudFront and Route 53
description: Content delivery network and DNS service for global content distribution
key_facts: CloudFront is a CDN that caches content at Edge Locations worldwide
Reduces latency by serving content from the nearest Edge Location
Route 53 is a scalable DNS service with 100% availability SLA
Route 53 supports routing policies: simple, weighted, latency-based, failover, geolocation
common_misconceptions: CloudFront only works with S3 (it can use any HTTP origin including EC2 and on-premises servers)
Route 53 is only for domain registration (it also provides DNS routing and health checks)

name: AWS Free Tier and Pricing Models
description: Free Tier types and fundamental AWS pricing concepts
key_facts: Three Free Tier types: Always Free, 12 Months Free, and Trials
Always Free: Lambda 1M requests/month, DynamoDB 25 GB storage
12 Months Free: EC2 750 hrs/month t2.micro, S3 5 GB, RDS 750 hrs/month
Pay-as-you-go: no upfront costs, pay only for what you use
AWS pricing varies by Region
common_misconceptions: The Free Tier never expires (12-month offers expire after the first year)
All services have a Free Tier (many services have no free tier)

name: Cost Management Tools
description: AWS tools for monitoring, analyzing, and optimizing cloud spending
key_facts: Cost Explorer visualizes and analyzes AWS spending over time
AWS Budgets sets custom spending alerts and forecasts
Billing Dashboard provides a high-level overview of current charges
AWS Pricing Calculator estimates costs before deploying resources
Cost and Usage Reports provide the most detailed billing data
common_misconceptions: Cost Explorer and Budgets are the same tool (Explorer analyzes past spending, Budgets sets future alerts)
AWS Pricing Calculator requires an AWS account (it is free and available publicly)
